More and more people own multiple devices and use them for digital asset sharing and collaboration.
Apps can support such use cases by implementing a data-sharing flow using Core Data and CloudKit.
This sample code project demonstrates how to use Core Data and CloudKit to share photos between iCloud users.
An iCloud container identifier is case-sensitive, and must be unique and begin with “ ”.
For an Apple Watch, use the Apple Watch app on the paired iPhone to confirm the settings.
CloudKit apps require a schema to declare the data types they use.
Apps that use can call to create the CloudKit schema that matches their Core Data model, or keep it up to date every time their model changes.
Apps must not call it in the production environment or in the normal development process that doesn’t include model changes.
After running the target, use to ensure each Core Data entity and attribute has a CloudKit counterpart.

For apps that use the CloudKit public database, use CloudKit Console to manually add the index for the field, and the and indexes for the field for all record types, including the type that Core Data generates to manage many-to-many relationships.
Use a reverse-DNS format for your identifier, as describes.
In the iCloud Capability Section, click the + button to create a new iCloud Container, or click a the checkbox next to an existing container you would like to use.
Select the CoreDataCloudKitDemoUnitTests target and use the Team pop-up menu to choose your developer team.
